questions.question: What is sound?
questions.answer: Sound is a form of energy produced due to vibration of a body and travels as waves through a medium.

questions.question: How does sound travel?
questions.answer: Sound travels as longitudinal waves through solids, liquids, and gases.

questions.question: What is the unit of frequency of sound?
questions.answer: The unit of frequency of sound is Hertz (Hz).

questions.question: Define pitch of a sound.
questions.answer: Pitch is the perception of how high or low a sound is, determined by its frequency.

questions.question: What is loudness?
questions.answer: Loudness is the perception of the intensity of a sound, related to the amplitude of vibrations.

questions.question: Name three types of musical instruments based on how they produce sound.
questions.answer: Stringed instruments (sitar), wind instruments (flute), percussion instruments (drum).

questions.question: What is the speed of sound in air at 0°C?
questions.answer: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is approximately 331 m/s.

questions.question: Define echo.
questions.answer: Echo is the reflection of sound from a surface back to the listener after a noticeable delay.

questions.question: What is reverberation?
questions.answer: Reverberation is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space due to multiple reflections.

questions.question: What is infrasound?
questions.answer: Infrasound refers to sound waves with frequency below 20 Hz, inaudible to humans.

questions.question: What is ultrasound?
questions.answer: Ultrasound refers to sound waves with frequency above 20,000 Hz, inaudible to humans.

questions.question: Give one application of ultrasound in daily life.
questions.answer: Ultrasound is used in medical imaging (sonography) to view internal organs.

questions.question: Name one application of infrasound.
questions.answer: Infrasound is used to detect earthquakes and volcanic eruptions.

questions.question: How does sound travel fastest: in solids, liquids, or gases?
questions.answer: Sound travels fastest in solids, slower in liquids, and slowest in gases.

questions.question: What is the audible range of humans in Hz?
questions.answer: The audible range of humans is approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

questions.question: Define amplitude of a sound wave.
questions.answer: Amplitude is the maximum displacement of particles in the medium from their mean position during vibration; determines loudness.

questions.question: What is a vibration?
questions.answer: Vibration is the rapid to-and-fro motion of a body about its mean position.

questions.question: Give one example of longitudinal waves in daily life.
questions.answer: Sound waves in air are longitudinal waves.

questions.question: Give one example of reflection of sound.
questions.answer: Echo heard from a wall is an example of reflection of sound.

questions.question: What is the function of the human ear?
questions.answer: The human ear detects sound waves and converts them into nerve signals for the brain.

questions.question: Name one instrument used to measure frequency of sound.
questions.answer: A tuning fork or frequency counter can be used to measure frequency of sound.

questions.question: Why does sound require a medium to travel?
questions.answer: Sound is a mechanical wave; it requires particles to vibrate and transmit energy, hence a medium is necessary.

questions.question: What happens to pitch if the frequency of sound increases?
questions.answer: If frequency increases, the pitch of the sound becomes higher.

questions.question: What happens to loudness if the amplitude of sound increases?
questions.answer: If amplitude increases, loudness of the sound increases.

questions.question: Define natural frequency of a body.
questions.answer: Natural frequency is the frequency at which a body tends to vibrate when disturbed without external force.
